g.data-narration: Welcome to Signal Functions! This module covers fundamental signal types and operations essential for understanding Signals and Systems in CSE.
A signal is a function that conveys information about a phenomenon. In engineering, signals represent quantities varying with time or space, like audio waves or sensor data.
Signals are foundational to communication systems, image processing, audio engineering, and control systems. They enable mathematical modeling of real-world phenomena.
Continuous-time signals are defined for every value of time t. Discrete-time signals are defined only at specific integer instants, denoted as x[n].
Periodic signals repeat after period T: x(t) = x(t + T). The fundamental period is the smallest positive T. Aperiodic signals show no repetition.
g.text: Periodic: x(t) = x(t + T) for all t

g.data-narration: Even signals satisfy x(-t) = x(t), symmetric about the y-axis. Odd signals satisfy x(-t) = -x(t), antisymmetric about the origin. Any signal can be decomposed.
g.text: x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t) - Any signal can be split!

g.data-narration: Energy signals have finite energy E but zero average power. Power signals have infinite energy but finite non-zero power. A signal cannot have both finite E and finite P.
g.text: ⚠️ Cannot have both finite non-zero E and P!

g.data-narration: The unit step function u(t) equals 0 for t<0 and 1 for t≥0. It's used to represent switching operations and create causal signals by multiplying with u(t).
g.text: Basic Signal: Unit Step Function u(t)
Used for: causality, switching, signal truncation

g.data-narration: The unit impulse δ(t) is zero everywhere except at t=0 where it's infinite, with integral equal to 1. The sampling property: ∫x(t)δ(t-t₀)dt = x(t₀).
g.text: Identity element for convolution: x(t) * δ(t) = x(t)

g.data-narration: Exponential signals are x(t) = Ae^(αt). When α is real, we get growing (α>0) or decaying (α<0) exponentials. Sinusoidal signals are periodic with frequency f.
g.text: Complex exponentials e^(jω₀t) form the basis of Fourier analysis!

g.data-narration: Time shifting x(t-t₀) moves the signal right by t₀. Time scaling x(at) compresses if |a|>1, expands if |a|<1. Amplitude scaling multiplies the signal by a constant.
g.text: x(t-t₀): right shift | x(at): compress if |a|>1 | A·x(t): amplitude multiply

g.data-narration: Example: x(t) = 3cos(5t) + 2sin(7t) is periodic because ω₁/ω₂ = 5/7 is rational. The fundamental period is T = 2π seconds.
g.text: Is it periodic? Find fundamental period.

g.data-narration: Example: Energy of x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n] equals Σ(0.25)^n from n=0 to ∞ = 1/(1-0.25) = 4/3. A geometric series sum formula applies.
Key formulas to remember: Energy and power calculations, even/odd decomposition, impulse sampling property, and periodicity condition for sinusoids.
That's Signal Functions! Remember: classify signals, understand step/impulse/exponentials, master operations, and practice energy/power calculations. Good luck!
